title: Bindhu vs Salomi on 11 November, 2025
body: Bindhu vs Salomi on 11 November, 2025 Author: Devan Ramachandran Bench: Devan Ramachandran MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 1 2025:KER:85691 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM PRESENT THE HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE DEVAN RAMACHANDRAN & THE HONOURABLE MRS. JUSTICE M.B. SNEHALATHA TUESDAY, THE 11TH DAY OF NOVEMBER 2025 / 20TH KARTHIKA, 1947 MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 AGAINST THE JUDGMENT DATED 07.08.2024 IN E.A.NO.87/2022 IN E.A NO.46/2021 IN E.P.NO.43/2018 IN OS NO.118 OF 2005 OF FAMILY COURT, KOTTARAKKARA APPELLANTS/CLAIM PETITIONERS: 1 BINDHU AGED 42 YEARS W/O.MOHANAN, CHARUVILA, AKKAVILA, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O, KOTTARAKKARA, KOLLAM DISTRICT, PIN - 691566 2 ROOTH AGED 62 YEARS W/O.BHASKARAN, CHARUVILA, AKKAVILA, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O, KOTTARAKKARA, KOLLAM DISTRICT - 691 566. REPRESENTED BY HER POWER OF ATTORNEY HOLDER DEVADAS B, AGED 49 YEARS, S/O.ROOTH, CHARUVILA, AKKAVILA, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O, KOTTARAKKARA, KOLLAM DISTRICT BY ADVS. SRI.ABDUL JAWAD K. SMT.A.GRANCY JOSE SMT.AYSHA A.A. RESPONDENTS/COUNTER PETITIONERS/DECREE HOLDERS AND JUDGMENT DEBTOR: 1 SALOMI AGED 62 YEARS W/O.DANIEL, PARAMBIL THEKKEKKARA, AKKAVILA MURI, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O, KOLLAM DISTRICT, PIN - 691566 MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 2 2025:KER:85691 2 LISY AGED 32 YEARS D/O.DANIEL, PARAMBIL THEKKEKKARA, AKKAVILA MURI, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O, KOLLAM DISTRICT, PIN - 691566 3 DANIEL AGED 69 YEARS S/O MATHAI, PARAMBIL THEKKEKKARA, AKKAVILA MURI, MYLOM VILLAGE, MYLOM P.O] KOLLAM DISTRICT, PIN - 691566 BY ADVS. SHRI.SHERRY J. THOMAS SRI.JOEMON ANTONY SHRI.ANTONY NILTON REMELO SMT.ANJANA P.V. SHRI.LIGISH XAVIER SRI N AJITH (AMICUS CURIAE) THIS MAT APPEAL (EXECUTION) HAVING COME UP FOR HEARING ON 3.11.2025, THE COURT ON 11.11.2025 DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING: MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 3 2025:KER:85691 DEVAN RAMACHANDRAN & M.B.SNEHALATHA, JJ. ------------------------------------------- MA(EXE)No.16 of 2024 ------------------------------------------- Dated this the 11th November, 2025 JUDGMENT M.B.Snehalatha, J In this appeal, appellants assail the order of the Family Court, Kottarakkara in E.A.No.87/2022 in E.A.No.46/2021 in E.P. No.43/2018, by which it dismissed their petition filed under Order XXI Rule 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure (for short, CPC ) 2. The case of the appellants is that by virtue of Ext.A1 sale deed dated 20.4.2007 of SRO Kottarakkara, Sri.Mohanan and late Lillykutty jointly purchased the petition schedule property having an extent of 23.47 Ares comprised in Resurvey No.26/9/2 in Mylom village from one Mr.Sreedharan. 1 st appellant/1st claim petitioner is the wife of Mohanan, who is abroad. 2 nd appellant/2nd claim petitioner is the legal heir of Lillykutty. Appellants/claim petitioners are in possession and enjoyment of the petition schedule property. On 02.07.2022, when a surveyor, an Advocate Commissioner and Amin deputed from the Family Court, Kottarakkara visited the petition schedule property, MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 4 2025:KER:85691 appellants/claim petitioners came to know that there was a suit as O. S.118 /2005 filed by R1 and R2 against R3 Daniel and they had obtained an ex-parte decree against R3 and in E.P.43/2018 in O. S.118 /2005, the Family Court, Kottarakkara executed a settlement deed No.1100/2020 of SRO Kottarakkara in favour of R2 - Lisy including the petition schedule property owned by appellants/claim petitioners covered by Ext.A1 sale deed. It was contended by the appellants/claim petitioners that their predecessor in title, Sreedharan, purchased the petition schedule property from R3 Daniel in the year 2004 and subsequently, in March 2007, Sreedharan and Daniel executed an exchange deed in respect of their respective properties. Though R3 had filed a suit as O.S.NO.97/2007 before the Sub Court Kottarakkara against Sreedharan, Mohanan and Lillykutty claiming ownership over the petition schedule property, the said suit was dismissed. The petition schedule property belongs to the appellants/claim petitioners. Hence, the claim petition is filed to set aside the attachment order in O. S.118 /2005 and in E.P.43/2018 and to release the claim schedule property from the attachment with a further relief to set aside the settlement deed No.1100/2020 of MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 5 2025:KER:85691 SRO Kottarakkara executed by Family Court, Kottarakkara in favour of R2 Lisy. 3. Respondents 1 and 2 resisted the claim petition, contending that the petition is not maintainable either in law or on the facts; that the claim petitioners or their predecessors in interest have not acquired any title or possession over the petition schedule property. According to respondents 1 and 2, the claim petition has been filed with the ulterior motive of causing obstruction to the delivery of the property in favour of R2 in the execution proceedings. R1 and R2 filed O. S.112 /2001 before the Family Court, Kollam, against R3 Daniel, claiming past and future maintenance and also claiming marriage expenses of R2. By order dated 07.04.2001, the Family Court, Kollam, attached the petition schedule property belonging to R3 under Order XXXVIII Rule 5 CPC . Subsequently, O. S.112 /2001 was transferred to Family Court, Kottarakkara, which was renumbered as O. S.118 /2005. While the said suit was pending before the Family Court, Kottarakara, R3 Daniel who was the sole defendant in the said suit, entered into a compromise with the R2 herein, namely the 2nd plaintiff in O. S.118 /2005 and he agreed to transfer his landed MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 6 2025:KER:85691 property, including the petition schedule property in favour of R2 - Lisy and accordingly, a compromise decree was passed on 29.09.2007 in O.S.No.118/2005. When the 3 rd respondent - Daniel who was the judgment debtor in O. S.118 /2005 failed to execute the conveyance deed in respect of the property as mandated in the compromise decree, 2 nd respondent - Lisy/decree holder filed E.P.No.43/2018 for getting the conveyance deed executed through the process of the Family Court and accordingly, on 16.07.2020 the Family Court executed the conveyance deed in respect of the petition schedule property in favour of 2nd respondent Lisy and thus Lisy obtained title and ownership over the petition schedule property. Since there was an attachment over the petition schedule property, R3 Daniel had no right to alienate the property in favour of Sreedharan in violation of the attachment order. Mohanan and Lillykutty, who allegedly purchased the property from Sreedharan have had knowledge of the attachment order in respect of the petition schedule property and they fraudulently obtained the sale deed by colluding with R3 Daniel and created documents by influencing the revenue officials. The alienation of the property allegedly done by R3 Daniel, which MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 7 2025:KER:85691 was under attachment, is barred under Section 64 CPC. The appellants/claim petitioners are not entitled to the reliefs sought in the claim petition. 4. The evidence consists of the oral testimonies of PW1 to PW3, RW1 to RW4 and documents marked as Exts.A1 to A10 series, Exts.B1 to B15 and Exts.X1 to X5. 5. After analyzing the evidence, both oral and documentary and after hearing both sides, the Family Court dismissed the claim petition filed by the appellants by the order impugned. 6. Heard the learned counsel for the appellants and the respondents 1 and 2. 7. The point for consideration in this appeal is whether the impugned order of the Family Court dismissing E.A.No.87/2022 in E.A.No.46/2021 in E.P.No.43/2018 in O.S.NO.118/2005 warrants any interference by this Court. 8. Appellants/claim petitioners claim title, ownership and possession over the property scheduled in the claim petition on the strength of Ext.A1 sale deed dated 20.04.2007 bearing No.1360/2007 of Principal SRO Kottarakkara. Ext.A3 is the tax MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 8 2025:KER:85691 receipt. According to them, Mohanan, the husband of the 1 st claim petitioner and Lillykutty, who is the predecessor in interest of the 2nd claim petitioner, jointly purchased the petition schedule property from PW2 Sreedharan by virtue of Ext.A1 sale deed. Ext.A2 is the prior title deed by which PW2 Sreedharan obtained the property. A perusal of Ext.A2 exchange deed dated 29.3.2007 would show that by virtue of the said document, R3 Daniel and his sister Kunjeli exchanged 23.47 Ares of property comprised in Resurvey No.26/9/2 in Block No.15 of Mylom Village. In exchange for the same, PW2 Sreedharan conveyed 21.50 Ares of property comprised in Resurvey No.395/6 and 395/6/2 owned by him. According to the claim petitioners, they purchased the property from PW2 Sreedharan by virtue of Ext.A1 sale deed dated 20.4.2007, and thereafter they are in possession and enjoyment of the same as the absolute owners of the said property. 9. The learned counsel for the appellant/claim petitioners contended that subsequent to the execution of Ext.A2 exchange deed dated 29.3.2007, R3 Daniel has had no right in the said property and therefore, he had no right to enter into a compromise with the plaintiffs in O.S.No.118/2005 agreeing to MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 9 2025:KER:85691 convey the property including the property exchanged to PW2 Sreedharan and therefore, the conveyance deed executed by the Family Court, Kottarakkara in favour of R2 Lisy on behalf of R3 Daniel in execution of the decree in O.S.No.118/2005 is not binding on them and the said document executed by the court in favour of R2 Lisy is liable to be set aside. 10. Per contra, the learned counsel for the respondents 1 and 2 took the attention of this Court to Exts.B1 to B3 documents and pointed out that on the date when Ext.A2 exchange deed was executed by R3 Daniel and his sister Kunjeli, there was an attachment in respect of the petition schedule property as per order in I.A.No.452/2001 in O.S No.112/2001 of Family Court, Kollam (which was subsequently transferred to Family Court, Kottarakkara and numbered as O. S.118 /2005) and therefore the alienation of the said property done by R3 Daniel is not binding on the decree holder in O. S.118 /2005, namely R2 Lisy herein. 11. Ext.B1 is the certified copy of the plaint in O.S.No.112/2001. Ext.B2 is the certified copy of I.A.No.452/2001 filed in the said suit under Order XXXVIII Rule 5 CPC and Ext.B3 is the certified copy of the attachment kaicheet. Exts.B1 to B3 MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 10 2025:KER:85691 documents would show that the said suit was filed by R1 and R2 herein, namely, the wife and daughter of R3 Daniel, against him seeking maintenance and marriage expenses. Along with the said suit, they had moved an application as IA.No.452/2001 seeking attachment of the property scheduled therein belonging to R3 Daniel, and by order dated 7.4.2001 of Family Court, Kollam, the attachment of the property was effected on 11.4.2001. Though O.S.No.112/2001 was subsequently transferred to Family Court Kottarakkara, the attachment over the property ordered by the Family Court, Kollam as per order in I.A.No.452/2001 continued and it was in force. Thus, Ext.A2 exchange deed dated 29.3.2007 executed by R3 Daniel in favour of PW2 Sreedharan was in contumacious violation of the order of attachment passed by the Family Court, Kollam in O. S. 112 /2001 which was subsequently transferred and numbered as O. S.118 /2005 of Family Court, Kottarakkara. Though the appellants/claim petitioners have raised a plea that PW2 Sreedharan purchased the property from R3 Daniel in the year 2004, such a document was not made available by them before the court for reasons best known to them alone. Appellants/claim petitioners or their predecessor in MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 11 2025:KER:85691 title namely PW2 Sreedharan had no right or interest or possession over the property attached by the court in I.A.No.452/2001 in O. S.112 /2001. 12. The learned counsel for the respondents 1 and 2 vehemently contended that in view of Section 64 of CPC any private transfer made by the R3 Daniel/judgment debtor in violation of the attachment shall be void as against all claims enforceable under the attachment. 13. For easy reference Sec.64 CPC is extracted here below: "64.Private alienation of property after attachment to be void. (1) Where an attachment has been made, any private transfer or delivery of the property attached or of any interest therein and any payment to the judgment-debtor of any debt, dividend or other moneys contrary to such attachment, shall be void as against all claims enforceable under the attachment. (2) Nothing in this section shall apply to any private transfer or delivery of the property attached or of any interest therein, made in pursuance of any contract for such transfer or delivery entered into and registered before the attachment." 14. The evidence on record would show that Ext.A2 exchange deed was executed when the order of attachment was in force. Thus, it is evident that R3 Daniel alienated the property in favour of PW2 Sreedharan in flagrant violation of the court order with an ulterior motive to defeat the claim of his wife and MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 12 2025:KER:85691 daughter who were the plaintiffs in O. S.112 /2001 (O. S.118 /2005). Where a property was transferred by the judgment debtor privately to a third party and the third party to another person during the substance of attachment, such transfer is void against claims enforceable under the attachment. Therefore, the transfer of the property in favour of appellants/ claim petitioners by virtue of Ext.A1 sale deed is hit by Section 64 CPC. 15. The appellants/claim petitioners would contend that they were unaware of Ext.B3 attachment order in respect of the property and they are bonafide purchasers for valuable consideration. It was also contended that in the encumbrance certificate issued from SRO, Kottarakkara, there is no entry regarding the attachment order in I.A.No.452/2001 in O.S.No.112/2001 of Family Court and thus they had no knowledge of the order of attachment. 16. Per contra, the learned counsel for R1 and R2 contended that even if there is no entry in the encumbrance certificate and even if the order of attachment was not communicated to the Sub Registrar Office, it does not invalidate MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 13 2025:KER:85691 the order of attachment as there is no mandate under Order XXI Rule 54(2) CPC that the order of attachment shall be communicated to the Sub Registrar Office. 17. As per Order XXXVIII Rule 11A CPC , the provisions of Civil Procedure Code applicable to an attachment made in execution of a decree shall, so far as may be, apply to an attachment made before judgment which continues after the judgment by virtue of the provisions of Order XXXVIII Rule 11 CPC . 18. Order XXI Rule 54(2) CPC reads as under: "2) The order shall be proclaimed at some place on or adjacent to such property by beat of drum or other customary mode, and a copy of the order shall be affixed on a conspicuous part of the property and then upon a conspicuous part of the court-house, and also, where the property is land paying revenue to the Government in the office of the Collector of the district in which the land is situate and, where the property is land situate in a village, also in the office of the Gram Panchayat, if any, having jurisdiction over that village." 19. As per Order XXI Rule 54(2) CPC the order shall be proclaimed at some place on or adjacent to such property by beat of drum or other customary mode, and a copy of the order shall be affixed on a conspicuous part of the property and then upon a MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 14 2025:KER:85691 conspicuous part of the court-house, and also, where the property is land paying revenue to the Government, in the office of the District Collector in which the land is situate and, where the property is land situate in a village, also in the office of the Gram Panchayat, having jurisdiction over that village. 20. Thus, from a reading of Order XXI Rule 54(2) CPC it is manifest that Order XXI Rule 54 CPC does not mandate that the order shall be communicated to the Sub Registrar Office. Nowhere in Order XXI Rule 54 CPC there is any requirement that the order must be communicated to the Sub Registrar Office. However, in practice, the courts sent a copy of the attachment order to the Sub Registrar concerned to make necessary endorsement in the concerned registers. This is not a statutory mandate but a precautionary administrative measure to prevent the defendant/judgment debtor from fraudulently alienating or encumbering the attachment property. It is a procedural safeguard adopted to protect the interests of the decree holder. Therefore, non communication of the order of attachment to the Sub Registrar Office does not invalidate the attachment. Therefore, even if the case of the appellants/claim petitioners is MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 15 2025:KER:85691 that in the encumbrance certificate issued from the Sub Registrar Office, the order of attachment in O. S.112 /2001 was not entered and therefore they are the bonafide purchasers is untenable. 21. It is in evidence that when O.S.No.118/2005 was pending before the Family Court Kottarakara, the parties to the said lis entered into a compromise pursuant to which Ext.B4 compromise decree was passed in the said suit. By virtue of Ext.B4 compromise decree, R3 Daniel/judgment debtor was bound to transfer the property referred to therein in favour of R2 herein, namely the 2nd plaintiff/decree holder in O.S.No.118/2005. It is in evidence that when R3 Daniel failed to execute the conveyance deed in respect of the property as mandated in the Ext.B4 compromise decree, the decree holders in O.S 118/2005, namely the respondents 1 and 2 herein, filed E.P.No.43/2018 to get the conveyance deed executed through the process of the court and accordingly the execution court executed Ext.B12 conveyance deed in favour of the decree holder Lisy namely, the R2 herein. It has come out in evidence that the property covered by Ext.B12 document had been mortgaged by R3 Daniel with the District Co- operative Bank, Kollam, and pursuant to the terms of Ext.B4 MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 16 2025:KER:85691 compromise decree, R2 Lisy discharged the said debt due to the said bank. The contention of the appellants/claim petitioners that they were unaware of the attachment of the property is unsustainable in law. R3 Daniel was bound by the order of attachment and therefore, the alienation made by him to PW2 Sreedharan by virtue of Ext.A2 exchange deed in brazen breach of the order of attachment and subsequent alienation made by PW2 Sreedharan in favour of Mohanan and Lillykutty are not binding on the decree holders in O.S.No.118/2005 namely R1 and R2 herein. 22. The learned counsel for the appellants/claim petitioners took our attention to Exts.A4 and A5 namely, the certified copies of the judgment and decree in O.S.NO.97/2007 of Sub Court, Kottarakkara and contended that though subsequent to the execution of Ex.A2 exchange deed, R3 Daniel and Kunjeli had filed a suit as O. S.97 /2007 before Sub Court, Kottarakkara against Sreedharan, Mohanan and Lillykutty claiming title over the property which was already transferred to PW2 Sreedharan, by virtue of Ext.A2 exchange deed, the said suit was dismissed by the Sub Court, Kottarakkara and the counter claim raised by MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 17 2025:KER:85691 Mohanan and Lillykutty claiming title, possession and prohibitory injunction was decreed in favour of Mohanan and Lillykutty and therefore R1 and R2 herein cannot dispute the title and possession of the appellants/claim petitioners. 23. A perusal of Ext.A4 and A5 judgment and decree would show that R1 and R2 herein, namely, the plaintiffs/decree holders in O. S.118 /2005 were not parties to O. S.97 /2007 and in the counter claim. Therefore, the judgment and decree in O. S.97 /2007 of Sub Court, Kottarakkara and the counter claim are not binding on R1 and R2/decree holders in O. S.118 /2005. Hence, there is no merit in the argument advanced by the learned counsel for the appellants/claim petitioners that in the light of the counter claim decree in O.S.No.97/2007 of Sub Court Kottarakkara in favour of Mohanan and Lillykutty the decree holders in O. S.118 /2005/R1 and R2 herein is barred from disputing the title of the claim petitioners/appellants and disputing the genuineness of ExtA2 exchange deed and Ext.A1 sale deed. 24. Moreover, the petition filed by the claim petitioners under Order XXI Rule 58 CPC to set aside the order of attachment in respect of the property and to set aside Ext.B12 settlement MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 18 2025:KER:85691 deed is not maintainable in law in view of the proviso to Order XXI Rule 58(1) CPC which reads as under: 58. Adjudication of claims to, or objections to attachment of, property "(1) Where any claim is preferred to, or any objection is made to the attachment of, any property attached in execution of a decree on the ground that such property is not liable to such attachment, the Court shall proceed to adjudicate upon the claim or objection in accordance with the provisions herein contained: Provided that no such claim or objection shall be entertained- a) where, before the claim is preferred or objection is made, the property attached has already been sold; or (b) where the Court considers that the claim or objection was designedly or unnecessarily delayed. " 25. From a reading of the proviso to Order XXI Rule 58 CPC it is manifest that no claim petition or objection under Order XXI Rule 58 CPC shall be entertained when the property attached has already been sold. In the case in hand, the property in respect of which the appellants/claim petitioners sought relief under Order XXI Rule 58 CPC has already been transferred in the name of R2 Lisy in execution of the compromise decree. Hence the Family Court ought not have entertained the claim petition under Order XXI Rule 58 CPC as it is hit by the proviso (a) to Order XXI Rule 58(1) CPC which provides that no claim petition or any objection made to the attachment of any property attached MA (EXE.) NO. 16 OF 2024 19 2025:KER:85691 in execution of a decree on the ground that such property is not liable to such attachment shall be entertained where before the claim is preferred or objection is made, the property attached has already been sold. Therefore, the relief sought by the appellants/claim petitioners to set aside the attachment and to lift the attachment and to set aside Ext.B12 settlement deed executed by the Family Court is unsustainable in law as rightly found by the Family Court and we find no reason to interfere with the order of the Family Court, dismissing E.A.No.87/2022. In the result, appeal stands dismissed with cost. While we conclude, we recognize the assistance offered by Sri.N.Ajith-learned Amicus Curiae, who has incisively analysed the law to assist us on the nuances of the provisions involved. We place on record our commendation for him. Sd/- DEVAN RAMACHANDRAN JUDGE Sd/- M.B.SNEHALATHA JUDGE ab
